sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaints & incident reporting** — no documented escalation path for injuries, class dissatisfaction, billing disputes, or safeguarding concerns; critical for duty of care and insurance
- **Coach performance management & capability** — no SOP for monitoring coaching quality, addressing substandard cueing/form correction, or managing underperforming coaches
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** — no SOP for daily/weekly cash handling, payment gateway reconciliation, refund approval, or audit trail
- **Health & safety incident response** — no formal process for near-misses, equipment failures, or emergency procedures (defibrillator, evacuation, injury on-site)
- **Member retention & win-back campaigns** — no documented playbook for at-risk member identification, pause-prevention outreach, or re-engagement sequences
- **Onboarding new coaches** — no structured induction covering brand standards, safeguarding, fitness assessment protocols, or probation checkpoints
- **Equipment maintenance & replacement** — no preventative maintenance log, inspection schedule, or replacement decision tree
- **Sick leave & absence cover** — no SOP for notifying members, rescheduling classes, or assigning cover coaches

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coach onboarding** — likely outdated on brand voice & class energy standards; missing fitness assessment sign-off and member feedback integration
- **Member complaint handling** — if it exists, check it covers non-injury scenarios (access issues, coach feedback, community concerns)
- **Class scheduling** — needs to factor in coach development rotation and peak-time cover protocols
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)** — review for consistency between email, blog, and paid ads; check brand tone is applied across all channels
- **Facilities SOPs** — add specific cleaning/hygiene standards for equipment post-use (especially post-

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF

## REVENUE — BIGGEST PRIORITY

**1. Arrest churn and rebuild active base — June's 0% churn is masking a larger structural issue**
- Active clients dropped 25 from May (122) to June (147) — that's a 15% headcount fall despite zero recorded churn, suggesting new joiners didn't stick or data lag. Even with relocation settled, you're losing momentum post-May.
- Target: Hit 160+ active clients by end of Q3 (you had 147 in bigger space — that's underutilised capacity). Offer 3-month loyalty bonuses at sign-up; implement 4-week check-in calls for all new members.
- Churn Q2 (May–June avg 2.87%) is creeping up vs Q1 post-relocation (May 5.74% down to June 0% is unstable). Lock in a target of **≤2% monthly churn**.

**2. Raise Average Revenue per Member (AR/M) from £120.48 — it's collapsing**
- June AR/M dropped to £120.48, down from £165.56 in May (27% fall). This is your single biggest red flag. Either members are downgrading plans or new joiners are on budget tiers.
- Action: Audit your pricing tiers immediately. If budget tiers dominate, stop selling them; push all new leads to a mid-tier class package + personal training upsell. Minimum target: £155 AR/M by August.
- Personal training uptake and class bundles are not visible in KPIs — introduce these metrics now.

**3. Capitalise on the larger studio — June revenue (£17.7k) is still below May (£20.2k) despite 147 active clients**
- The new studio should support higher utilisation and revenue per sq ft. You're paying for 2x space but not filling it.
- Action: Launch a "New Studio Celebration" package (3-month lock-in at £45/month, 10 classes/month + 1 PT session). Target: convert 30 of your 147 members to this by month-end. Projected revenue uplift: £1,350.

---

## EXPENSES — FOCUS ON NON-PEOPLE COSTS

**4. Non-people expenses surged and are eating profit — June £9,390 is the 2nd highest all year**
- Compare Q2 avg (May–June: £9,312/mo) to Q1 (March £15.

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is overloaded at 133 sessions.** She's carrying 56% of total delivery while also managing head coach responsibilities. Redistribute her Personal Training (44 sessions) — Eilis can take more 1:1s on Wed/Thu/Fri to free JoJo for group classes and strategic work.

• **Grace is significantly under-used at 52 sessions.** Despite being founder, she's only coaching Mondays. Consider adding a Wednesday or Friday Build: Lower or Elevate class (her strengths) to increase studio revenue and take pressure off JoJo.

• **Build: Upper is over-dependent on JoJo (15 of her sessions).** Add one Eilis-led Build: Upper class on Wed/Thu to spread the load and give JoJo more breathing room.

• **Eilis has capacity to grow.** She's at 48 sessions with availability on Wed/Thu/Fri. Increase her Personal Training uptake and add a second regular class (e.g. Elevate or Plate) to balance the timetable and develop her confidence.

• **Body Composition Scans are fragmented across all three coaches (40 total).** Consolidate these into dedicated time slots with one assigned coach per day to improve efficiency and free capacity for group classes.

• **Coverage gaps on Wed/Thu/Fri are exposed.** Without Grace or consistent Eilis presence mid-week, members have limited options. Commit Grace to one mid-week slot and build Eilis's Wed/Thu schedule to plug gaps.

class_stats.suggestion:
**Move low-attendance classes to peak days.** Schedule Engine, Capacity, and Barbelle on Wednesday or Tuesday mornings/evenings when member traffic is highest—this gives these underperforming classes a better chance of reaching critical mass for motivation and community.

**Add extra Build class slots on Wednesday.** Your Build classes dominate attendance across both periods. Wednesday is your busiest day but may be hitting capacity; adding a second Build session (different time or style variation) could capture demand you're currently losing.

**Consider dropping or restructuring Saturday offerings.** Saturday attendance is 40% below Monday despite being a traditionally popular gym day. Either consolidate Saturday classes into fewer, high-energy sessions to build habit, or test a completely different format (outdoor, social, beginner-focused) to discover what your members actually want on weekends.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — Execution Plan for Grace

• **Start with your existing data, not a blank sheet.** Pull last 3 months of transactions/inquiries. What are people actually *asking* for and *buying*? Build your menu around real demand, not what you think you should offer. This saves time and avoids guessing.

• **Separate the two menus physically.** Coach menu = what they're empowered to sell/offer (packages, add-ons, referral incentives, upsells). Member menu = clean, simple pricing for new enquiries. Use different formats (coaches get internal spreadsheet with margins; members get visual one-pager). Coaches need the business logic; members need clarity to decide.

• **Price anchoring first.** Pick your premium tier (e.g., "6-month unlimited + 2 nutrition consults") and price it at what you'd *like* to earn. Work backwards to entry-level. Don't price low first—you'll regret it. Test pricing with 2-3 existing members before finalizing.

• **Include the "friction killers" only.** What stops people from joining or upgrading? Answer those explicitly on the member menu (e.g., "Flexible pause option," "First session free," "Bring a friend discount"). These are your real differentiators, not features.

• **Share drafts with 1-2 coaches + 1-2 members this week.** One-sentence feedback each: "Would you buy/sell this?" Adjust based on objections, then lock it. Done over perfect.

What's your current price range for memberships?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Execution for The Forge

• **Audit your current drop-off points first** — Use Google Analytics to find where visitors leave (homepage, pricing, class details). Record 3-5 real people going through your site cold and note what confuses them. This tells you exactly what to fix, not guesses.

• **Create a "questions map" from real sources** — Pull your top 20 FAQ emails, Instagram DMs, and trial call objections. Group them by theme (price concerns, class types, women-only value prop, beginner-friendliness, etc.). Your landing page copy should *directly answer* these in order of frequency.

• **Lead with your unique angle, not features** — Don't say "strength classes, cardio, yoga." Say something like "The only gym in Belfast built for how women actually train — no mirrors, no intimidation, just results." Then slot in your FAQs under that narrative so each section answers a likely objection.

• **A/B test one high-impact element this week** — Pick either your headline, your trial CTA button text, or your pricing transparency section. Change one thing, drive 50-100 clicks through each version using paid ads or email, measure conversion. Ship the winner.

• **Make your offer embarrassingly obvious** — A single, clear call-to-action (free trial, pricing, book a tour). No decision fatigue. One per page section maximum.

What's your biggest current objection from people who almost sign up?

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Execute Your Feedback System—Here's How:

• **Start stupidly small this week**: One A4 laminated feedback card at reception + one QR code linking to a 60-second Google Form (3 questions max: "What's working?", "One thing to improve?", "Would you recommend us?"). Don't wait for "perfect"—launch Monday.

• **Make it a habit, not a hassle**: Integrate into your existing touchpoint. Post-class? Embed a 20-second verbal ask as members grab water—"Quick feedback takes you 30 seconds, helps us get better." Offer a monthly prize draw (€25 coffee voucher) to drive compliance.

• **Close the loop publicly**: Share one piece of feedback + your action on your WhatsApp group or in-gym notice board each week ("Member said: wanted 6am class. We're piloting it in March."). This shows you actually listen. Changes behavior.

• **Route responses to one person (you)**: All feedback goes to your email/spreadsheet. You review every Friday for 15 mins, spot patterns. If 3+ members mention the same thing, it's a priority signal—not a random opinion.

• **Turn insights into quick wins**: Implement ONE change per month that directly traces back to feedback. Members see cause-and-effect. They'll keep giving you gold.

What's your biggest concern about getting honest feedback from members?

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Plan for The Forge

**1. Start with your revenue-tier hierarchy, not alphabetical order**
Write SOPs in order of: *Membership* → *Personal Training* → everything else. Why? These two generate 80%+ of your operational friction and customer complaints. You'll build momentum and see immediate operational wins before tackling ancillary services.

**2. Use a "Day 1 Customer" lens, not internal jargon**
Don't write what staff *think* customers should do. Shadow a real new member for one full week across each service—map exactly where they get confused, where they ask questions twice, where they waste time. Your SOP should eliminate those friction points specifically.

**3. Create one "SOP template" with these non-negotiable sections only:**
- **Trigger** (what initiates this service)
- **Customer journey steps** (what they do, in order)
- **Staff decision points** (where staff judgment matters—be explicit)
- **Failure points** (what goes wrong—and how to recover)

Skip the corporate fluff. Each SOP should be 1-2 pages max. If it's longer, you haven't understood the process yet.

**4. Embed pricing/upsell clarity into each SOP immediately**
Within each service SOP, hardcode *when* and *how* you mention Fuel Forward, Well75, Strongevity. Make it a handoff, not an afterthought. (This is where most gyms leak revenue.)

**5. Test and iterate fast—assign one person ownership**
Pick one SOP (Personal Training). Have that person live it for 2 weeks, record what breaks, re

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar: Execution Plan for Grace

**Do this TODAY to lock it in:**
- Pick ONE platform first (Instagram or TikTok—where your members actually spend time). Don't split focus across five channels. Master one, then expand.
- Use a free tool (Buffer, Later, or even a Google Sheet) and schedule the next 4 weeks RIGHT NOW. Block 90 minutes this week to batch-create content—film 12 videos/posts in one session, then drip them out. This kills the "what do I post?" paralysis.

**Content formula that actually works for women's fitness:**
- 40% member wins (before/afters, testimonials, transformation clips—members tag friends, algorithm loves it)
- 30% behind-the-scenes (you training, gym energy, team banter—builds community, not salesy)
- 20% educational (form tips, nutrition myths, workout breakdowns—positions you as expert, drives DMs)
- 10% promotional (class promos, membership offers—only this much, or people unfollow)

**Make it SUSTAINABLE (so you actually stick to it):**
- Assign posting time (e.g., Tue/Thu 6:30 AM, Sat 9 AM) when your members scroll. Set a phone reminder.
- Batch-film monthly: one Sunday afternoon = all your content for 4 weeks. Done.
- Track what hits (likes, saves, DMs) weekly—ruthlessly kill posts that flop, double down on what converts.

**Quick win this month:** Post one member success story every week. Members share it → reach explodes → new sign-ups cost you nothing.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing Videography Sessions with Jordan

• **Lock it in NOW as a standing appointment** — Book all 8-week slots for the next 12 months in Jordan's calendar today. This removes the friction of rescheduling and forces consistency. Set it for the same day/time each cycle (e.g., third Tuesday, 10am). Grace tends to deprioritize content; making it unmovable prevents drift.

• **Batch-shoot 4-6 pieces per session, not one** — With Jordan on-site, get testimonials from 2-3 members, film a workout demo, capture behind-the-scenes, and shoot one class in action. This gives you content for 8 weeks of posts without paying per-shoot rates. Creates efficiency Jordan will appreciate too.

• **Give Jordan a one-page shot list 5 days before** — Specific angles, which classes to film, which members are good on camera, what problem each video solves (new member anxiety, class variety proof, transformation story). This isn't busywork—it means Jordan delivers usable content instead of raw footage you have to hunt through.

• **Assign ONE person (not Grace) as the content coordinator** — They brief Jordan, manage logistics, handle editing/uploading, and track what's working. Grace reviews final cuts only. This prevents bottlenecking and keeps sessions actually happening instead of getting bumped for firefighting.

• **Tie it to a specific growth metric** — e.g., "New members citing 'saw you on Instagram' hit 15% of sign-ups." Measure before first shoot, check quarterly. If it's not moving the needle, adjust the content strategy—not the schedule.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# 6-Week Trial Automation Overhaul for The Forge

• **Audit the breakdown NOW** — Map exactly where enquiries drop off (website form → email response → booking → first session → week 6 check-in). Grace likely has leaks at 2-3 points. Fix those before adding automation—automating a broken process is pointless.

• **Create a 3-email sequence, not 6** — Day 1: Welcome + gym tour booking link (Calendly embedded). Day 10: "How's week 2 going?" + highlight one class she'd enjoy based on intake data. Day 35: "Last week to lock in your plan" + membership pricing. Keep it tight—women are filtering out noise, not craving frequent contact.

• **Segment by source and engagement** — Trialists from Instagram ads behave differently than referrals. Use Zapier/Make to tag enquiries by source, then serve different automations. High-engagement trialists get faster upsell messaging; hesitant ones get more value-first content.

• **Build a simple win metric into the sequence** — Track "% who complete first session" and "% who convert at week 6" separately. If conversion is weak, the problem isn't automation—it's the trial experience itself. Automate *around* the real friction point, not past it.

• **Implement in Typeform → Mailchimp/Klaviyo** — Grace likely uses one already. Takes 2-3 hours to build; test with 10 real enquiries before rolling out. Get this live in the next 7 days while she has momentum.

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist + KPI Ownership — Execution Blueprint

**1. Reverse-engineer from revenue/retention, not job titles**
Don't start with "what does JoJo do?" Start with: Which 3-4 metrics move The Forge's needle (member acquisition, retention rate, class utilization, revenue/member)? Assign ONE primary KPI per person based on their leverage point, not their role. This prevents vague checklists and creates accountability with teeth.

**2. Make the weekly checklist *absurdly* specific to their role**
- **JoJo (assuming operations/retention):** "Text 3 at-risk members + log responses" / "Update class attendance tracker by Wednesday" — not "manage members"
- **Eilis (sales/new members):** "Contact 5 warm leads from referral/social" / "Update pipeline spreadsheet" — not "grow membership"
- **Sarah (assume coaching/experience):** "Deliver 2 new-member onboarding sessions" / "Gather 3 member feedback snippets for testimonials"

Each person's checklist should take 2-3 hours max. If it's longer, you've dumped your job onto them.

**3. Define the ONE metric each person owns (and they report it Friday 4pm)**
Pair each role with a single, trackable number they influence directly. JoJo owns *retention rate (%)*. Eilis owns *new member signups (#)*. Sarah owns *class attendance rate (%)* or *member satisfaction score*. Frame it as: "Your job is to move this number 5% this month."

**4. Document it

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

• **Pre-session prep (do this before anyone arrives):** Send each team member their individual 90-day targets + one specific metric they own 48 hours early. Frames the conversation around accountability, not just listening. When they arrive, they've already mentally committed.

• **Lead with the SSO first, not last:** Spend 10 mins on your Single Serving Objective — what ONE outcome matters most in 90 days (e.g., "hit 250 members" or "5-star Google rating"). Make it visceral. Then show how their lane directly feeds it. They need to see *why* their job matters, not just *what* it is.

• **Assign ownership + decision rights explicitly:** Don't say "you'll handle marketing." Say "You own all member communications — you decide email cadence, tone, and content without approval. You report back on open rates weekly." Clarity kills confusion. Ambiguity kills execution.

• **Build in a commitment moment:** At the end, have each person state back their lane and their 90-day win in 1-2 sentences. Public commitment (even in a small room) is behaviorally binding. You'll see follow-through jump.

• **Document it in writing same day:** One-pager per person: their lane, 2-3 key projects, success metrics, and when you check in. Slack it immediately. No "I thought you said..." three weeks later.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge

• **Map actual hours, not job titles** — Have each team member (coaches, front desk, cleaners, managers) log what they're *actually doing* for 1 week in 15-min blocks. You'll spot the real bottlenecks (e.g., "Sarah's doing class scheduling + member complaints + equipment ordering"). Job descriptions lie; time logs don't.

• **Identify the "invisible load" kills** — Ask directly: who's staying 30 mins late? Who's skipping breaks? Who's handling multiple people's overflow work? These people are your over-stretched red flags and usually won't volunteer it.

• **Create 3 rebalance tiers for day 1–7, 8–21, 22–30** — Don't move everything at once (kills morale/consistency). Day 1: stop new tasks hitting over-stretched staff. Days 8–21: delegate specific low-impact tasks to under-utilized people (with training). Days 22–30: full restructure roles. This staggers disruption.

• **Assign one person *ownership* of each core function** — Not shared responsibility. If "member experience" is everyone's job, it's no one's. Post it visibly so the team sees the rebalance is intentional, not punishment.

• **Run a 15-min all-hands after day 30** — Show the new structure, acknowledge who picked up what, and explicitly ask: "Is this sustainable for you?" One more honest conversation saves you from right-sizing badly.

# 10% Cost Cut for The Forge — Specific Execution

**Hit the big three first (likely 60-70% of savings):**
• **Staff scheduling**: Audit actual peak hours vs. current shifts. Shift to variable staffing (part-time/on-call) for off-peak times. If you run 10 full-time staff, even moving 2-3 to flexible hours at your slowest times saves £800-1,200/month.
• **Utilities**: Get an energy audit done (often free from suppliers). Women's gyms are heating/AC heavy — reprogram thermostats by 1-2°C, upgrade to LED lighting on a phased basis. Target £300-500/month.
• **Equipment maintenance**: Switch from monthly service contracts to quarterly + DIY checks using YouTube tutorials for basic issues. Most gyms overbuy this.

**Quick wins (implementation this week):**
• **Supplier renegotiation**: Ring your 3 biggest vendors (cleaning supplies, equipment, toiletries) with a simple line: *"I'm consolidating to one supplier and want your best price for 12 months guaranteed."* Most will move 5-12% immediately to lock you in.
• **Cancel or downgrade unused subscriptions**: Gym management software, unused software licenses, over-specified broadband plans. Check your last 3 months of bank statements — usually £200-400 hiding there.

**Reality check**: Before cutting, model which cuts directly hurt member experience (don't touch class frequency or cleanliness). Cuts that leak into retention cost you more than the savings.

What's your current team size and approximate monthly overhead?

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — Execution Plan for Grace

• **Lock in a recurring 90-minute block on the same day each month** (e.g., first Friday after month-end close). Non-negotiable. Use your phone's calendar blocking feature NOW so this doesn't slip. Assign one person (you or trusted staff) as the single owner — ambiguity kills consistency.

• **Build a one-page KPI dashboard template in Excel/Google Sheets TODAY** with only YOUR critical metrics: member revenue, churn rate, class utilization %, payroll as % of revenue, and cash runway. Pre-format it so you just plug numbers in—no rebuilding the wheel monthly. 10 minutes of setup saves you 40 minutes of admin each month.

• **Create a 15-minute decision rule:** Once KPIs are updated, immediately identify ONE metric that's off-track and ONE quick action to fix it (e.g., if churn spiked, schedule retention calls that week; if utilization dropped, run a flash promotion on off-peak classes). This forces KPIs to drive decisions, not just sit in a file.

• **Pull data sources the day before your KPI session** (payment processor, membership software, accounting system). Don't waste your 90 minutes hunting for numbers—have your admin or a team member compile the raw numbers into one document first.

• **Share key findings with your team in 5 minutes flat** (top 3 metrics + 1 action plan). Keeps everyone aligned and holds you accountable to follow through.

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Start with 24 months of actual data** — Pull your last 2 years of membership numbers, churn rates, class utilization, and average revenue per member by cohort (new vs. retained). This is your baseline. Without it, you're guessing. Spend max 2 hours mining your POS/membership system this week.

• **Build 3 scenarios, not 1** — Conservative (15% growth), Base (30% growth), Aggressive (50% growth). For each, model: membership growth rate, class pricing/add-on revenue, fixed costs (rent, staff), and seasonal dips. Use a simple Google Sheet with 12 monthly rows. This forces you to spot where you'll actually hit constraints (space, trainers, equipment).

• **Identify your ONE money lever first** — Is it class pack upsells? Corporate memberships? Retention improvements? Model what happens if you move that needle 10%. This tells you where to invest energy first, not spreading thin across everything.

• **Set trigger points for action** — Define now: "If membership growth hits 80 members/month, we hire staff X." "If churn exceeds 8%, we pause new acquisition and audit classes." This prevents reactive panic and keeps growth deliberate.

• **Monthly review ritual (30 mins max)** — Compare actual vs. forecast on the 5th of each month. One column: variance. One column: action needed. Share with your team. This keeps forecasting alive and useful, not a one-time exercise.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Execute Service-by-Service P&L for The Forge

**Get granular on revenue attribution RIGHT NOW:**
- Pull last 3 months of transactions and tag EVERY line item: which class type, PT session, or add-on (shakes, apparel, etc.). If your system doesn't allow this, use a simple Google Sheet with manual entry for 2 weeks to stress-test the model. You need actual numbers, not estimates.

**Build a brutally simple P&L with 4 columns per service:**
Revenue | Direct Costs (instructor pay, product COGS) | Allocated Overhead (rent ÷ capacity use %, utilities, software pro-rata) | Net Profit Margin. This takes 2 hours max. Don't overthink allocation—use headcount percentage or hours-used as your denominator.

**Identify the profitability ranking TONIGHT:**
Which service prints money? Which subsidizes others? This ranking drives everything—pricing, scheduling, marketing spend next.

**Test one hypothesis immediately with data:**
If PT sessions show 40%+ margin but only fill 60% of slots, Grace's next 7 days should be: audit *why* they're not full (messaging? price? quality?), then fix that lever before adding new services.

**Action this week: share results with your most honest staff member**
They'll spot where your assumptions about costs are wrong faster than any analysis. You need reality-check, not perfection.

What's your current revenue split between these three buckets—rough percentages?

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations — Here's What To Do TODAY

**1. Export your full automation library RIGHT NOW**
Pull a complete list from your platform (Zapier, Make, ActiveCampaign, whatever you're using). Group by trigger type — you'll spot duplicates immediately when you see "new enquiry → send welcome email" listed 3 times. Delete the oldest versions, keep the one with the most steps. Takes 20 mins, saves chaos.

**2. Map your actual enquiry-to-trial journey on paper**
Trace ONE real customer from form submission → trial booking. Note exactly what emails/SMS fire, when, and what action prompts each step. Your automations should mirror this 1:1 with zero gaps. If there's a 48-hour radio silence, your automation chain is broken — fix it.

**3. Test with a fake enquiry from YOUR phone number**
Fill out your form with a test email. Watch what lands in your inbox, in what order, and how fast. If anything's missing (e.g., no booking link, no calendar reminder) or delayed (emails arriving at 3am), your prospects are dropping off here.

**4. Identify the ONE bottleneck killing conversions**
Is it: no follow-up if they don't book immediately? Broken trial booking link? Automations not tagged correctly so some enquiries slip through? Fix this bottleneck first — it's costing you more than duplication.

**5. Schedule a 90-minute quarterly cleanup into your calendar**
Don't audit again in 6 months. Set a recurring task. Takes 45 mins each time, prevents this mess building up again.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Specific Execution

**1. Identify your 3 tasks ruthlessly (do this today)**
Look at your last week: billing/invoice follow-ups, class booking confirmations, and member check-in surveys when they go inactive. These are repetitive, time-sinks, and don't need human judgment. Ignore "it would be nice to automate" — pick tasks costing you 5+ hours weekly combined.

**2. Start with the easiest win: Automated booking confirmations**
Use Zapier + your booking system (Mindbody/Zen Planner) to send SMS/email confirmations immediately after sign-up. It takes 15 mins to set up, removes admin work, and **reduces no-shows by 20-30%** (verified in fitness). Template it: "Hi [Name], you're booked for [Class] Tuesday 6pm. Reply CONFIRM or we'll release your spot."

**3. Build a lapsed-member re-engagement sequence**
Set Zapier to trigger a 3-email drip when someone hasn't booked in 10 days. First email: "we miss you" + class schedule. Second (3 days later): social proof ("10 members joined this month"). Third: hard offer ("50% off month back"). This automates your outreach and catches revenue leakage before it happens.

**4. Automate invoice chasing (the annoying one)**
If any member's payment fails, trigger an automated email + Slack notification to you within seconds. That's faster intervention = fewer lost payments. Template: "Hi [Name], your payment didn't go through. Update here: [link]. Reply

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website – Action Plan

• **Get an SSL certificate TODAY** – Use Let's Encrypt (free) or your hosting provider's one-click install. This takes 10 minutes and protects customer payment data. Non-negotiable before taking any online bookings or payments.

• **Audit your current vulnerabilities in 2 hours** – Run your site through Qualys SSL Labs (free) and OWASP ZAP scanner. You'll get a prioritized list of actual problems to fix, not theoretical ones.

• **Password-protect your admin dashboard immediately** – If you're using WordPress or similar, change default login URL, enforce strong passwords for all staff accounts, and enable two-factor authentication. This stops 90% of attacks on small gyms.

• **Set up automated backups (not optional)** – Configure daily backups to an external drive or cloud service. If you get hit with ransomware, you recover in hours instead of losing weeks of booking data and member info.

• **Require HTTPS for member login pages** – Any page where members enter passwords or payment info needs encryption. Most hosting providers flag this now, but confirm it's active for your booking/payment checkout.

**Skip the expensive "security audit" reports for now.** Grace needs the critical stuff locked down fast. Revisit with a professional once you're generating serious revenue and handling high member volumes.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

- Your cost-per-lead has tripled in two months (£6 → £20) whilst lead volume has collapsed 70% (69 → 21), despite lower spend. This screams creative fatigue or audience saturation—the same ads are exhausted and relevance scores are dropping.
- Close rate has halved (29% in Apr → 48% in May → 48% in Jun sales/leads, but your actual conversion is 10/21 = 48% in June). Wait—your June close rate (48%) is actually your best on paper, but you're only converting 10 of 21 leads. In April you converted 20 of 69 (29%). The issue isn't your sales team; it's lead *quality* tanking as cost-per-lead rises.
- Your May spike (£14/lead, 23 sales) was your goldilocks month. June's retreat below May spend didn't help—lower budget meant less testing room and you hit the same tired audience. The platform is telling you to either refresh creative or expand targeting, not cut spend.

**Actions**

1. **Pause underperforming creatives immediately and test 4 new angles next week**: Static before/after transformation posts (proven for fitness); testimonial video from a recent convert (social proof at scale); problem-agitate-solve carousel on "busy mum" objections; and a 15-sec reel on studio atmosphere/community (not just fitness). Broad creative fatigue is killing CPL—variety matters more than perfection here.

2. **Expand your audience targeting beyond your current core**: You've saturated the obvious (women 25–45, fitness interest). Test: lookalikes from your 48% converters only (not all leads), broader age bands (18–24 and 46–55), and interest stacking away from "gym" (e.g., wellness + busy professionals + local community groups). Your Apr volume came from somewhere fresher.

3. **Increase budget back to £550/month but ring-fence £100 for testing new audiences/creatives**: June's £417 was a false economy—you can't test at scale on that. May proved £500+ works if creative is fresh
